user_query:
I'm a local radio journalist in Saint‑Pierre and Miquelon prepping a quick‑reference sheet for on‑air updates (weather warnings, ferries, official notices). Using the input file at input/targets.csv, please build a small, verifiable catalog of current official source URLs, check their availability from the command line, and rank them for reliability.

Requirements:
1) For each target_id in input/targets.csv:
   - Perform at least one live search engine query to find at least one current source URL that matches the description and org_hints. Prefer official websites over social media. Record every search you run in logs/search_log.jsonl (one JSON object per line with fields: target_id, search_engine, query, timestamp_iso, notes).
   - Collect 1–3 candidate URLs per target_id. Avoid duplicates.

2) For every discovered URL, run an HTTP check from the shell using curl (or an equivalent standard CLI) and capture both success and error details:
   - Append the raw command output (including stderr) for each URL to logs/fetch_log.txt. Do not delete error messages; they must remain visible in the log.
   - Also produce a machine‑readable results/fetch_results.csv with columns: target_id, discovered_url, final_url (after redirects if available), http_status (integer or NA), curl_exit_code (integer), error_category. Classify error_category as:
     • success if exit code=0 and 200–299;
     • redirect if exit code=0 and 300–399;
     • client_error if exit code=0 and 400–499;
     • server_error if exit code=0 and 500–599;
     • ssl_error if exit code is 35 or 60;
     • dns_error if exit code is 6;
     • timeout if exit code is 28;
     • other otherwise.

3) Build results/sources_raw.csv with one row per discovered URL and these columns: target_id, discovered_url, domain, tld, source_org_guess, matched_keywords (semicolon‑separated), official_match (0/1). Compute domain and tld from the URL. Set official_match=1 if the URL contains at least one keyword from the official_domain_keywords field for that target_id (case‑insensitive); list all matched keywords in matched_keywords.

4) Create results/sources_ranked.csv by joining sources_raw with fetch_results on (target_id, discovered_url), then filtering out exact duplicate URLs. Rank rows per target_id by:
   - official_match descending (1 before 0),
   - availability_score descending, where availability_score=2 for success, 1 for redirect, 0 for all other categories,
   - domain ascending (alphabetical) as a final tie‑break.
   Include a rank column (1..n within each target_id) and keep all other columns from the join.

5) Write a concise results/summary.md that, for each target_id, lists the top‑ranked URL with its error_category and a one‑sentence justification referencing the matched official keyword(s) and availability.

Minimum checks:
- At least one URL must be found and processed for every target_id (respect min_sources in the CSV). If any command fails, keep the failure in logs/fetch_log.txt and categorize it in fetch_results.csv per the mapping above.
- All output paths must exist exactly as specified: logs/search_log.jsonl, logs/fetch_log.txt, results/fetch_results.csv, results/sources_raw.csv, results/sources_ranked.csv, results/summary.md.

Notes:
- Do not hardcode URLs into the code; you must actually perform search engine queries and document them in logs/search_log.jsonl.
- Prefer official websites (e.g., government, meteorological service, territorial authority, airport/port operator) aligned with org_hints. If a social channel is the only official presence you can find, include it but it will likely be ranked lower by official_match.
- Keep the process reproducible and locally verifiable from the produced artifacts. All file paths are relative to the workspace directory.
